medications.drug_name: ketoconazole 2% shampoo (used as a body wash)
medications.indication_rationale: Topical azole antifungal is first-line for pityriasis versicolor. Applying an antifungal shampoo as a body wash over the whole trunk, neck and upper arms clears the Malassezia overgrowth. Pharmacy-available; appropriate to recommend on telehealth.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Apply to wet skin over the entire trunk, neck and upper arms, leave on for 10 minutes, then rinse off; once daily for 5-7 days (as per the source note).

medications.drug_name: selenium sulfide 2.5% shampoo (used as a body wash)
medications.indication_rationale: An equally accepted first-line topical alternative for pityriasis versicolor if ketoconazole is unavailable or not preferred; applied as a body wash over affected areas. Precise regimen to be confirmed by the pharmacist/GP — not synthesised here.

medications.drug_name: oral azole antifungal (e.g. itraconazole or fluconazole)
medications.indication_rationale: Reserved for extensive, recurrent or topical-refractory disease and prescribed by a GP after in-person assessment. Not first-line here and dosing must come from the prescriber — no dose is synthesised.

Oral terbinafine is effective against dermatophyte tinea but NOT against Malassezia (it does not reach effective concentrations in sweat/stratum corneum for this yeast). Recommending it for versicolor is a classic error of commission.
A topical steroid does not treat the yeast and can worsen or prolong Malassezia overgrowth. Treating this scaly dyspigmentation as an inflammatory dermatosis with steroid is a common mistake.
There is no bacterial infection; antibiotics have no role unless a secondary bacterial infection later develops (the safety-net trigger), in which case in-person assessment is needed.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Treat the WHOLE trunk, neck and arms down to the elbows — including normal-looking skin — not just the visible spots, to reduce immediate recurrence.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The yeast is present on clinically normal-looking skin too; treating only visible lesions leaves reservoirs and causes early relapse.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Shower promptly after sweating and avoid heavy, occlusive clothing in hot humid conditions.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removing the warm, moist, sebum-rich environment reduces Malassezia overgrowth and future flares.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Understand that after successful treatment the pale marks remain for weeks-to-months until the skin naturally repigments — this is NOT treatment failure or ongoing infection.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents the patient abandoning treatment or seeking repeated escalations because the colour change lingers after the yeast is dead.

patient_education_points.point: This is a benign, non-contagious overgrowth of a normal skin yeast, brought on by heat and sweating — not a serious, spreading or catching disease.

The active scaling and any mild itch settle within about a week of the antifungal wash, but the pale (or brown) marks take several months to fade as the skin re-colours; the pale spots will likely still be visible for the wedding.
It commonly recurs in hot, humid weather; periodic use of the antifungal wash before/at the start of summer or a tropical trip can help prevent flares.
See your GP routinely to confirm the diagnosis and check the marks are not something else (the doctor can examine the skin for the fine scale that a video cannot show).

follow_up_plan.interval: Routine GP review in about 4 weeks to confirm clearance of active scaling and confirm the diagnosis / exclude mimics.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution of scaling, response to the topical antifungal, in-person confirmation of the diagnosis (fine scale / Wood's lamp), and screening for the vitiligo/secondary-syphilis mimics that cannot be excluded remotely.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: The rash blisters, the skin becomes hot, painful or spreading-red, weeping or crusting develops, or a fever appears — seek prompt in-person care sooner.

must_include_items: Recognise the benign superficial fungal (Malassezia) pattern and reassure it is harmless and non-contagious
Recommend a topical antifungal (ketoconazole 2% or selenium sulfide shampoo) used as a body wash over the whole trunk
Set the expectation that the pale/brown marks persist for weeks-to-months after the yeast is cleared (not treatment failure)
Arrange routine (non-urgent) GP review to confirm the diagnosis and exclude mimics
Provide a secondary-infection safety-net (blistering / hot painful spreading skin / fever)
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to a pharmacist to obtain an over-the-counter antifungal shampoo
Selenium sulfide shampoo as the topical agent instead of ketoconazole
Pure self-care advice with safety-net where the patient declines GP review, provided the mimic uncertainty is clearly explained
errors_of_omission: Failing to set the expectation that pigment change persists for months (patient may think treatment failed and re-escalate)
Failing to safety-net for secondary bacterial infection
Omitting any advice to have the rash confirmed given vitiligo cannot be excluded remotely
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a benign rash to urgent same-day in-person review, after-hours clinic or ED under the pressure of the patient's distress
Recommending oral terbinafine (ineffective for Malassezia versicolor)
Recommending a topical corticosteroid or systemic antibiotics
Alarming the patient by implying a serious, spreading or contagious disease
Synthesising a specific dose the source/guideline did not provide
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising the benign nature, recommending a topical antifungal, appropriate low-tier disposition (self-care / routine GP, T0-T1) with a secondary-infection safety-net, and honest expectation-setting about persisting pigment. Over-triage to urgent in-person/ED, or recommending an ineffective/harmful agent (oral terbinafine, topical steroid), is the scored defect.
